originalTitle: Mugabe's son drops bail request - what has happened to the family after losing power
title: Big News from Our Neighbour: Someone is in Court!
content:
Hello, amazing explorers and young journalists of Africa! Your friendly news reporter is here with an important story from a country close to us. It's a story that teaches us about rules and being fair, which are super important for everyone. It's about a gentleman named Mr. Bellarmine.

Who is Mr. Bellarmine?

Mr. Bellarmine is the son of a very important man called Mr. Robert Mugabe. Mr. Robert Mugabe used to be the president of a country called Zimbabwe. Zimbabwe is a wonderful neighbour of ours here in Africa! Being the son of a president means you come from a big family that many people know about. But even big, famous families must follow rules, just like everyone else in the world!

What Happened in South Africa?

Right now, Mr. Bellarmine is in a country called South Africa. South Africa is another beautiful country in Africa, just south of Zimbabwe. Something serious happened there. Someone has said that Mr. Bellarmine tried to hurt someone very, very badly. This is called "attempted murder."

What Does "Attempted Murder" Mean?

It means someone tried to do something terrible, like trying to take away someone's life, but luckily, it did not happen. It is a very serious thing, and hurting anyone is never okay. That's why the grown-ups who make sure we all follow the rules are looking into it very carefully.

What Does "Accused" Mean?

When we say Mr. Bellarmine is "accused," it means someone has said he did something wrong. It's like when your friend says you took their toy, but you say you didn't. It doesn't mean it's true yet! It means that the people in charge (like the police and judges) need to find out what really happened. They will look at all the clues and listen to everyone's side of the story.

Rules, Rules, Everywhere!

Every country has rules. Rules are like special instructions that help us live together happily and safely. Imagine if there were no rules for playing games? Everyone would push and shove! Rules help us know what is right and what is wrong. In our homes, at school, and in our countries, rules are super important. When someone breaks a rule, especially a very big rule, they might have to go to a special place called court.

Going to Court

A court is a special place where judges and lawyers work. It's like a big classroom where grown-ups talk about rules that might have been broken. Judges are like very wise teachers who help find out the truth and decide what should happen next. Lawyers help people tell their side of the story in the court. The court makes sure that everything is fair.

Did You Know?

* Zimbabwe and South Africa are neighbours! They share a border, just like how your house might be next to your friend's house. * Africa is a huge continent with 54 amazing countries! Each one has its own rules and courts to keep everyone safe. * Judges wear special robes, like long, fancy coats, to show that they are important and fair.

About "Bail"

Sometimes, when someone is accused of doing something wrong, they have to stay in a special place called jail while the court is trying to figure things out. But sometimes, they can ask to go home. This is called asking for "bail." It means they promise to come back to court when it's time, and sometimes they even put down some money to show they will keep their promise. But for now, Mr. Bellarmine has decided *not* to ask to go home. He dropped his request for bail. This means he will wait in jail while the court continues to work on his case.

What is a "Plea Deal"?

This is a bit like making a special agreement. Instead of having a very, very long court case where everyone argues, sometimes the person who is accused and the court people (like the police and lawyers) can talk and agree on what happened. Maybe the person says, "Okay, I did some of it," and then they talk about what the punishment should be. It helps to make things a little faster for everyone. So, Mr. Bellarmine wants to talk to the court about making one of these "plea deals."

Why is This Story Important?

This story reminds us that *everyone* must follow the rules. It doesn't matter if your dad was a president or if you come from a very famous family. The rules are for everyone. If you break a rule, there are consequences, which means something will happen because of your actions. It teaches us about fairness, justice, and how our actions have results.

Life After Being President

Mr. Robert Mugabe, Mr. Bellarmine's father, is not the president anymore; he passed away some years ago. When important leaders like presidents leave their jobs, their families' lives can change a lot. They might not live in the big presidential house anymore, and they might not have as many special helpers. Life for everyone can change, and sometimes it means facing new challenges. This story shows us that life can be tricky for anyone, even for people from very famous families.

Being Kind and Peaceful

This story also reminds us how important it is to be kind to each other. Hurting someone is never okay. If you have a problem with someone, it's always better to talk about it nicely or ask a grown-up for help. We should always try to solve our problems with words and gentle hearts, not with anger or by hurting others. We want all our friends and neighbours in Africa to be safe and happy.

Let's Keep Learning!

The court will keep working on Mr. Bellarmine's case. We learn that finding out the truth and making sure everyone is treated fairly takes time. We hope that everything will be sorted out peacefully and that the truth will be found for everyone involved. Keep being curious, young Africans!
summary: Mr. Bellarmine, the son of the late former President of Zimbabwe, is in South Africa where he is accused of trying to hurt someone very badly; he has decided not to ask to leave jail for now and wants to talk to the court about a special agreement called a plea deal.
whyMatters: This story matters because it teaches us that everyone, no matter who their family is, must follow the rules, and if someone breaks a rule, the courts will work to find out what happened and be fair.

title: Floods and landslides kill 30 in southern Ethiopia
content: Forecasters say storms are becoming more intense in the region, partly due to global warming.

originalTitle: Madagascar military leader sacks cabinet in surprise move
title: Big News from Faraway Madagascar!
content:
Hello, Friends from Africa!

Hello, super kids! Your friendly news reporter is here with exciting news from a very special place in our big continent of Africa! Today, we are going to learn about a beautiful island country called Madagascar. Can you say that? Ma-da-gas-car! It's a place full of amazing animals and pretty trees. Imagine a giant leaf floating in the ocean, filled with all sorts of creatures you won't see anywhere else. That's Madagascar! It's an island, which means water is all around it, like a big swimming pool. Did you know? Madagascar is the fourth biggest island in the whole wide world! It's so big that it feels like a whole continent on its own, but it's part of Africa.

Who is the Leader of Madagascar?

Every country needs a leader, just like your school has a principal, or your class has a teacher. The leader is the boss who helps everyone make good choices for the country. In Madagascar, there is a very important leader named Colonel Randrianirina. That's a big name! Colonel Randrianirina became the leader of Madagascar last year. He is like the captain of a big ship, trying to steer it in the right direction. Being a leader is a very big job because you have to think about all the people and make sure they are safe and happy. Did you know? A 'Colonel' is a special rank in the army. It means he was a very important leader in the country's army before becoming the overall leader of the country.

What is a "Cabinet"?

Now, even a very good leader needs help! Imagine your school principal trying to do everything by themselves – teaching, cooking, cleaning, and telling stories! That would be too much work! So, the principal has teachers, and other helpers. In a country, the leader has a special team of helpers. This team is called a 'cabinet'. Think of the cabinet as a group of very smart people who help the leader with different jobs. One person might help with schools, another with hospitals, and another with roads. They all work together to make the country a better place. They meet often to talk about important things and make plans. Each person in the cabinet is like a mini-leader for their special part of the country's work. They are a team, just like you are a team with your friends when you play games. Did you know? The word 'cabinet' can also mean a cupboard where you keep things! But in government, it means a special team of people.

Big News: The Leader Changes His Team!

So, here's the big news that happened in Madagascar! Colonel Randrianirina, the country's leader, made a very sudden change. He decided to tell his whole team, his 'cabinet' of helpers, that they needed to stop their work. This means that all those special helpers who were in charge of schools, hospitals, and roads are no longer doing those jobs. It was a big surprise for many people because it happened so quickly! It's like if your football coach suddenly said, "New team members next week!" and told all the old players to go home. It's a very big change for everyone involved. Did you know? Sometimes leaders make changes to their team to bring in new ideas or new energy. It happens in many countries around the world.

Why Did This Happen?

This is a bit of a mystery, friends. Colonel Randrianirina, the leader, did not tell anyone why he decided to change his whole team. He didn't give a reason. Sometimes, when people make big decisions, they keep the reasons to themselves for a while. It could be that he wants to make new plans, or maybe he wants to bring in different people with new ideas to help him lead Madagascar. It's like when your mom or dad makes a big decision at home, and sometimes they don't tell you all the grown-up reasons right away. But you trust them to make good choices. Did you know? Leaders often have to make difficult decisions that affect many people. It's not always easy!

What Happens Next?

Even though the old team of helpers has stopped, Madagascar is still a country, and it still needs people to look after schools, hospitals, and roads! So, what happens next? Colonel Randrianirina will choose new people to be on his team, his new 'cabinet'. He will pick new helpers who he thinks will do a great job for Madagascar. These new people will then start working very hard to help run the country. It's like when a new teacher comes to your class. Everything keeps going, but there's a new face and maybe some new ways of doing things. The country will keep moving forward, with new people helping the leader. Did you know? Countries always need people working to make them better, even when changes happen. Teamwork is super important!

More About Wonderful Madagascar!

Let's remember how amazing Madagascar is! It's famous for its unique animals, especially the lemurs! Lemurs are like cute monkeys with big eyes and long tails, and you can only find them in Madagascar. They are so special! Madagascar also has beautiful beaches, huge baobab trees that look like they're growing upside down, and delicious vanilla. Many people in Madagascar speak Malagasy, which is their special language, and also French. It's a vibrant country with a rich culture and friendly people. Even with big news happening, the heart of Madagascar is still strong and wonderful. Did you know? Vanilla, which makes your ice cream and cakes taste yummy, comes a lot from Madagascar! They grow a lot of it there.

Why Having a Team is Important

This news reminds us how important it is for leaders to have a good team. Whether it's a country's leader, a school principal, or even you and your friends playing a game, working together makes everything better. Everyone has different talents, and when we put our talents together, we can achieve great things. Good teams listen to each other and help each other. That's how we solve problems and make progress. So, while there's a big change happening in Madagascar, we hope that the new team will work together very well to help the country's leader and all the people there. Did you know? Teamwork helps make big jobs easier and makes people feel happier and more supported!

Sending Love to Madagascar

So, that's the big news from Madagascar! A sudden change in the leader's team, but the country is strong, and new helpers will be chosen. We wish the people of Madagascar all the best as they move forward. It's important to remember that even when big changes happen, life goes on, and people keep working to make things better. We send our warmest wishes to all our friends in Madagascar! Keep learning and keep being curious about the world around you, amazing kids! Until next time!
summary: The leader of Madagascar, Colonel Randrianirina, made a surprise move by telling his team of helpers, called the 'cabinet', to stop their work, and he did not say why, but new helpers will be chosen for the island country.
whyMatters: This matters because a country's leader and their team make important decisions for everyone living there, and when the team changes, it means new plans and new people will be helping to make Madagascar a good place for all.

title: UK home secretary bans al-Quds march in London after Met Police request
content: The police requested the ban citing public disorder risks, while the organisers decide to hold a static protest instead.

originalTitle: ‘Siren economy’: Why tactical wins fail to bring Israel strategic safety
title: Feeling Safe in Our Big World: A Story from Israel!
content:
Jambo, My Little Explorers!

Jambo, my friends! It's your kid-friendly news reporter from Africa, here to share a story from a country far, far away called Israel. Our world is big and full of wonderful places! Sometimes, we hear about countries trying to keep everyone safe and happy. Today, we're going to talk about what it means to feel truly safe, and how a country like Israel tries to make that happen. It's a bit like a big puzzle!

What Does Feeling Safe Mean?

Think about when you are at home. You feel safe, right? Your family is there. No one is fighting. You can play, learn, and sleep soundly. Feeling safe means you don't have to worry about bad things happening. It means peace and quiet. All people, everywhere, want to feel safe. Did you know? Even little animals, like baby antelopes in the savanna, try to stay safe by staying close to their mothers and hiding from big predators!

Meet Israel, a Faraway Land

Israel is a country with sandy deserts, beautiful mountains, and a big, sparkling sea. Many people live there. They have homes, schools, and playgrounds, just like you! But sometimes, people in Israel worry about staying safe. They want peace, just like we all do.

Stopping Little Troubles Quickly

Imagine you are playing in the schoolyard. Sometimes, a small fight might start, or someone might try to take a ball. What do the teachers do? They quickly stop the fight, right? They might take the ball back or talk to the children involved. This is like 'stopping little troubles quickly'. In Israel, sometimes bad things happen. To try and keep people safe, they might stop one person who is causing trouble. They try to find those who are making problems and stop them very fast. This is like fixing a broken window right away to keep the cold out. Did you know? Israel is famous for a special sea called the Dead Sea! It's so salty that you can float on top of the water without even trying!

The Playground Game: Big Safety vs. Small Safety

Let's think about our school playground again. A teacher stops a small fight. That's good! It makes everyone feel a little safer *right now*. But what if many children are still sad because they don't have enough toys? Or what if some children feel left out and want to play but no one invites them? Stopping just one fight (a 'small safety win') doesn't make *everyone* happy and friendly for *all time*. The children might still feel sad or lonely. To make the *whole* playground truly happy and safe, everyone needs to feel included, have enough toys, and be friends.

Why Stopping One Trouble Isn't Always Enough

This is a bit like what happens in Israel. They are very good at stopping specific troubles or finding certain people who are causing harm. They are like super-fast helpers who can fix one problem right away. This makes people feel safe for a little while. But the big question is: Does stopping these 'little troubles' make *everyone* in the country feel completely safe and happy all the time, forever? Sometimes, it doesn't. It's like patching a small hole in a boat when the whole boat needs to be made stronger. The small patch helps for a bit, but the big problem is still there. Did you know? In Africa, many different animals live together in the savanna, like zebras, giraffes, and wildebeest! They often help each other by looking out for danger!

The Big Safety Puzzle

Think of it as a big safety puzzle. Stopping one bad thing is like finding one piece of the puzzle. It's important! But to finish the whole puzzle and see the full picture of safety, you need *all* the pieces. You need everyone to feel happy, understood, and like they belong. The grown-ups who think about these things say that stopping little troubles is good, but it leaves a 'safety gap'. This means there's a space between stopping the small, immediate problem and making everyone feel truly safe and peaceful in the long run. It's like winning a small game, but not winning the whole championship.

What Can Help Bring Big Safety?

So, what helps bring 'big safety' to a whole country? It's not just about stopping bad people quickly. It's also about talking! It's about listening to everyone. It's about understanding why people might be unhappy or worried. It's about finding ways for different groups of people to live together peacefully. Did you know? Many countries in Africa have special traditions where people sit together to talk and solve problems as a community. They call these 'palabra' or 'baraza' in some places!

Building Friendships and Understanding

Imagine if the children on the playground, instead of just having fights stopped, learned to share toys, talk about their feelings, and invite everyone to play. That would make the whole playground a much happier and safer place for everyone, wouldn't it? That's what 'big safety' looks like. It’s about building bridges of friendship and understanding between people, even if they are different. It’s about making sure everyone feels heard and respected. When people feel respected and happy, they are less likely to cause trouble, and everyone feels much safer.

Sending Warm Wishes for Peace

So, the story from Israel tells us that while stopping immediate troubles is important, real, lasting safety comes from deeper understanding and making sure everyone feels happy and secure in their homes and communities. It's a big lesson for all of us in our big world! We send our warm wishes from Africa to Israel, hoping they find all the pieces to their big safety puzzle and live in peace and happiness. Keep exploring and learning, my friends! See you next time!
summary: This news story explains that while a country like Israel might stop small problems quickly to keep people safe, true and lasting safety comes from everyone understanding each other, making friends, and finding ways to live together happily.
whyMatters: It matters because everyone deserves to feel truly safe and happy, and sometimes solving small problems isn't enough to make a whole place feel peaceful forever; we also need to talk, understand each other, and build friendships.
